reviews.text: Absolute weapon on the race course. The edge grip on hardpack is unreal — these skis lock in like they're on rails. Very stiff, not forgiving at all if you get in the backseat. Short turn radius makes them incredibly quick gate to gate. Not a ski for cruising around, purely a race tool.

Best slalom ski I've ever been on. The connection to the snow through the plate binding system is incredible. You feel every bump and ridge, which is exactly what you want in a race. Stiffness is a 10/10, no chatter at any speed. Definitely need strong technique to handle these.

Incredible precision for racing, but completely inappropriate for casual skiing. Edge hold on ice is phenomenal. Turn initiation is very demanding — you need to commit fully. Light for a race ski. I use these only for training slalom.

Bought these for masters racing and they deliver. Crazy edge grip on icy race courses. The 155 is super snappy, almost too quick if you're not ready. Very responsive underfoot. Zero float in any kind of soft snow but that's not what they're for.

The race sandwich construction with the double titanal layers makes this ski incredibly damp despite being light. No vibration even at full speed. The 66mm waist means you're edge to edge in milliseconds. Pure on-piste performance.

Massive GS ski that demands respect. At 183cm these are a handful but the stability at 80+ km/h is breathtaking. Full camber profile gives you the most powerful edge grip available. Extremely stiff — the stiffest ski I've ever tested. Not playful at all but that's the point.

The ultimate on-piste weapon. These skis reward aggressive skiing with the most precise turns you'll ever make. The 19m turn radius means you need space to open them up. Damp, stable, confidence-inspiring at speed. Absolutely zero forgiveness for sloppy technique.

Technically the best ski in the Atomic lineup. Edge grip is perfect, the plate system gives incredible power transmission. Heavy compared to the S9i but that mass translates to stability. Only for strong, aggressive skiers. Will punish intermediates.

Took these for a GS training day and they absolutely rip. The longest radius in my quiver but when you let them run they are incredibly satisfying. The dampest race ski I've used — absorbs everything. Available up to 183cm which is great for tall racers.

Too much ski for general resort use. I bought them thinking they'd be a sporty carver but they're really a full race ski. On groomed they're unbelievable but I can't take them anywhere else. Stiff as a board, zero powder float, and very heavy.

My first pair of skis and they're perfect for learning! Very forgiving when I make mistakes. Light enough that my legs don't get tired. The tip rocker helps me initiate turns without much effort. Soft flex makes it easy to control at slow speeds. Love these for groomed runs.

Coming back to skiing after 15 years and these are ideal. Soft enough to be forgiving but not so soft they feel floppy. Good on groomed terrain, the 78mm waist is just right for frontside skiing. Don't try to go fast — they get chattery above moderate speeds.

We recommend these to our adult beginners all the time. The rocker/camber profile makes turn initiation very easy. Light weight reduces fatigue in lessons. Not a ski you'll grow into — most people outgrow them in 2-3 seasons as they get more aggressive. But for starting out, hard to beat.

Exactly what I needed. Easy turns, soft flex, lightweight. I can ski all day on groomers without my legs dying. The integrated binding keeps things simple. Not great when it gets bumpy or icy but on nice groomed runs they're wonderful. Very playful feeling.

Great value beginner ski. Very soft flex is easy to handle. Edge grip is acceptable on groomed but struggles on hard snow. Light and maneuverable. The 78mm waist is a good width for learning — not too narrow, not too wide. Only downside is they feel unstable at speed.

Solid beginner ski that we put a lot of people on. Durable construction, forgiving flex pattern. Clients report easy turn initiation and good control at low speeds. The rocker tip really helps nervous beginners. Not much dampness — transmits vibrations at speed.

The Supershape line is the gold standard for expert carving. The e-Speed takes it up a notch with the electronic dampening. Incredibly damp — the smoothest ride on hardpack I've found. Edge grip is phenomenal, just a tick below a pure race ski. Stiff but manageable for strong intermediates.

Demoed everything on the mountain and these were the clear winner. The graphene construction keeps the weight reasonable while the dampness is extraordinary. You feel connected to the snow but protected from chatter. Expert-level carving performance with about 10% more forgiveness than a race ski.

My dream carving ski. The 72mm waist transitions edge to edge beautifully. Holds on ice better than anything short of a race ski. Very stable at high speed — I was laying trenches at full tilt. Not playful at all, this is a serious carving tool. The 184cm is available for taller skiers.

Top-tier on-piste ski. The dampness from the graphene sandwich is the standout feature — absorbs everything. Stiff enough for aggressive skiing but not quite race-level demanding. Edge grip and stability are both near perfect. Only loses a star because it's one-dimensional — no off-piste ability.

If you only ski groomers and you ski hard, this is THE ski. Damp, grippy, stable, precise. The construction quality from Head is first-rate. I wish it had a bit more versatility but that's not what it's designed for. Camber profile means no rocker, no float, pure carving.

Perfect for East Coast hardpack and ice. The edge grip and dampness combo is exactly what you need when the surface is bulletproof. Stiff enough to power through crud but smooth enough that your teeth don't rattle. My go-to for icy days.

Solid frontside ski with that Volkl precision. The titanal layers give it real dampness and stability. Edge grip on hardpack is excellent — not quite race-level but very strong. Stiff enough for aggressive skiing, the 76mm waist makes it quick edge to edge.

Love this on the ice coast. The camber profile and titanal construction give you the edge grip you need on hard snow. Damp and stable, doesn't chatter. The Deacon line hits the sweet spot between race and recreational. Available in 178cm which works great for my height.

Very precise carving ski. Not quite as damp as the Supershape but more lively feeling. Edge grip is strong on groomers. Camber-only profile means no tip rocker, so it can be a bit grabby in soft snow. Best kept on piste.

I use these on groomer days. Excellent edge hold, good dampness from the titanal, and a solid stable platform at speed. Not forgiving if you're not centered — the camber profile demands good balance. Not the ski for bumps or trees, purely a carving tool.

The Deacon 76 is Volkl's answer to the Head Supershape. Slightly less damp but a bit more playful. Edge grip is nearly identical. Good stability, stiff flex. The plate binding adds precision. A strong choice for piste-focused skiing.

The best all-mountain ski I've ever ridden. 100mm waist gives you enough float for powder days but it still carves on groomers. The titanal makes it damp and stable at speed. Handles crud and chop like a champ. Stiff enough for experts but not punishing. If I could only have one ski, this is it.

Incredibly versatile. I took these from groomed morning corduroy to afternoon powder stashes and they handled everything. Rocker-camber-rocker profile floats in soft snow and grips on hard. Good playfulness for an all-mountain ski. The 186cm is a beast in big terrain.

Moved from Vermont to Colorado and needed one ski for everything. The Enforcer 100 does it all. Edge grip on hardpack is surprisingly good for 100mm. Damp and stable in chop. Decent float in powder. Not a specialist in anything but competent everywhere.

Solid quiver-of-one option. The stiffness and dampness are impressive for an all-mountain ski. At 100mm waist it's wider than a frontside ski but manageable on groomers. The rocker profile keeps it friendly in soft snow. Good stability at moderate to high speeds.

Took these into everything — bumps, steeps, trees, groomers. They handle it all with confidence. The titanal construction gives excellent dampness for chattery conditions. Not the lightest ski but the weight adds stability. Playful enough to throw around in tight spots.

If you want one ski, the Enforcer 100 is the safe choice. Good edge grip, good float, good stability. Not great in deep powder (for that you want wider) and not razor-sharp on ice (for that you want narrower). But 90% of the time, it's exactly right.

The Rustler 10 is the most fun ski I've ever ridden. Incredibly playful — you can butter, spin, and smear with ease. The rocker profile floats well in powder and the 102mm waist is wide enough for most conditions. Not super stable at mach speed but that's not the point. This ski wants to play.

Bought this as my resort freestyle ski and it delivers. Pop off everything, spin easily, land softly. The flex is medium-soft and very even, great for park and natural features. Floats decently in powder. Edge grip is adequate but not its strength. Pure fun machine.

More playful than most all-mountain skis. The Rustler sacrifices some stability and edge grip for fun factor and it's worth it. I can ski all over the mountain and have a blast. Not the ski for icy days or pure carving, but everywhere else it shines.

Perfect in tight trees. Light enough to maneuver quickly, playful enough to navigate around obstacles, wide enough to float when it's deep. The rocker tips release easily from turns. My go-to for tree skiing and natural features.

Very popular on demo days. People love the playful feel. Not a charger — gets unstable above moderate speeds. But for having fun all over the mountain, it's hard to beat. Good powder float, acceptable edge grip. The Blizzard construction quality is excellent.

Great ski for intermediate-to-advanced riders who want to explore the whole mountain. Forgiving enough to learn on but capable enough to grow into. The playfulness encourages you to try new things. Not the most damp — you feel the bumps. But the fun factor is off the charts.

The QST 106 is Salomon's best freeride ski. Carbon and flax construction gives it a unique damp but lively feel. Floats beautifully in powder at 106mm. Handles variable snow conditions with ease. The rocker-camber-rocker profile is well-tuned for off-piste terrain.

Great option for resort freeride. The carbon construction keeps weight down while flax adds natural dampness. Good float in powder, decent edge grip for traverses and hardpack sections. Playful enough for drops and airs. Not as stable as heavier chargers in high-speed chop.

My powder day ski at the resort. 106mm waist with the rockered tips means I'm floating from the first run. The flax construction adds a nice smooth feel without being dead. Good for trees and steeps. Edge grip is fine for linking turns on harder snow between powder stashes.

Versatile freeride ski that handles most conditions well. Not as specialized as a pure powder ski but much better on hardpack than a fat powder board. The QST 106 is a good choice for people who ski 60% off-piste, 40% on-piste. Damp, playful, floaty.

Innovative construction with the carbon/flax layup. You get dampness without the weight penalty of titanal. Ride quality is smooth and composed. Float is excellent for 106mm. Turn radius is 20m so you need some space but it's manageable. The 188cm is available for big skiers.

The Mindbender 108Ti is a charging machine. The titanal makes it stiff and damp — this is not a playful noodle. At 108mm it floats well in powder but the real story is how it handles high-speed crud and chop. Incredibly stable. If you ski fast and aggressive in variable terrain, this is your ski.

My daily driver at Jackson Hole. These skis eat up the gnarly, wind-affected, variable snow that we get. Stiff, damp, and incredibly stable at speed. The 193cm is a beast but at 6'3" I need the length. Floats in powder, grips on hardpack traverses. A true big-mountain weapon.

One of the best freeride skis we've tested. The titanal sandwich construction gives it elite-level dampness and stability. Edge grip is strong for a 108mm ski. Demanding to ski — not for intermediates. The weight is noticeable on the chairlift but worth it on the descent. Available up to 193cm.

If you want a ski that powers through the worst conditions, this is it. Heavy but that mass becomes an advantage in crud and chop. Stiff flex keeps you stable even when the terrain is trying to throw you around. Decent playfulness for a charger. Not as nimble as lighter alternatives.

My competition freeride ski. The combination of stiffness, dampness, and width is perfect for big mountain lines. You can charge through anything. The rocker-camber-rocker profile balances float and grip. At 186cm it's powerful but manageable. The best freeride charger I've used.

Solid freeride option from Dynastar. The M-Free 108 is well-balanced between float and grip. Titanal construction gives good dampness. Not as stiff as the Mindbender but more playful. Good for the skier who wants to charge but also have fun. Available up to 192cm for tall riders.

Good all-around freeride ski. Floats well at 108mm, decent edge grip, stable in variable conditions. Not outstanding in any single category but competent everywhere off-piste. The playfulness is nice — lets you throw in some style. Dampness is good, not exceptional.

My go-to for Verbier's varied terrain. Handles the steep couloirs, the tracked-out powder fields, and the groomed home runs. The 108mm waist is the sweet spot for European freeride. Stable enough for speed, playful enough for tight spots. Dynastar knows freeride.

Compared this head-to-head with the QST 106 and the Enforcer. It splits the difference — more playful than the Enforcer, more stable than the QST. Float is excellent. Edge grip is acceptable. Weight is moderate. If you want a versatile freeride ski, it's a solid choice.

Good ski for advancing from groomed-only to exploring off-piste. The width gives confidence in deeper snow and the rocker makes turn initiation easy. Not as demanding as the K2 Mindbender but still rewards good technique. A good step up for ambitious intermediates.

The Atris is a powder dream. At 108mm with generous rocker, it floats effortlessly. The poplar and fiberglass construction is light and snappy. More playful than heavy titanal powder skis. You can surf, slash, and throw spray with this ski. Edge grip on hard snow is mediocre but that's not what it's for.

Brought these to Japan and they were perfect. Incredible float in the deep stuff, playful enough to navigate tight trees, light enough for long powder days. The feel is surfy and fun. Not great on groomed — gets a bit squirrelly. But in powder, nothing makes me smile more.

Black Crows makes the most beautiful skis and the Atris might be the best. The big rocker sections in tip and tail give unreal float. Very forgiving for a powder ski — you don't need expert technique. Playfulness is incredible, you can butter and smear everywhere. Love this ski.

Excellent powder ski but I wish it had a bit more stability at speed. In deep snow it's magical — effortless float, great playfulness, fun surfy feel. But when the snow gets tracked out and variable, it can get bounced around. Not as damp as titanal alternatives. Still my favorite for powder days.

I recommend the Atris to clients who want a dedicated powder/freeride ski. It excels in soft snow — great float, playful ride, forgiving behavior. Edge grip is weak on hardpack so you need a second ski for groomer days. Weight is moderate, not a touring ski but manageable for resort use.

The Pagoda Tour 112 RP is the ultimate powder touring ski. At 112mm waist with full rocker, the float is unmatched. The carbon/paulownia construction makes it shockingly light for its width — under 1800g per ski. Uphill efficiency is great. Downhill in powder is pure bliss. Edge grip on firm snow is minimal.

My backcountry go-to. Light enough for long approach tours, wide enough for the deepest days. The full rocker profile means incredible float but not much grip on hard traverses. DPS quality is outstanding. The 184cm handles beautifully in open bowls. Soft flex, very forgiving.

Switched from splitboarding and the DPS Pagoda was my first touring ski. The width and float made the transition easy. Extremely playful and surfy in powder. Very light weight for the width. On hard snow it's like skiing on butter — no edge at all. But I pick my days.

Great powder touring ski but limited to soft conditions. The full rocker and soft flex mean minimal performance on hard or icy snow. Float is a 10/10 — the best I've experienced at any width. Weight is impressive for 112mm. If you tour primarily for powder, this is the one.

DPS makes premium skis and the Pagoda Tour is their masterpiece. Incredibly light carbon construction. The 112mm waist with full rocker provides effortless float. Very playful and forgiving in powder. On anything firm, prepare to slide sideways. A specialist's ski for deep days.

Moment makes incredible skis and the Wildcat 108 is their masterpiece. The maple/triax construction gives it a unique blend of power and playfulness. Stiff enough to charge, playful enough to throw tricks. Float is excellent at 108mm with rocker. Made in the USA, which I appreciate.

My everyday ski at Snowbird. The Wildcat handles the steep and deep with authority. More chargy than the Atris but more playful than the Mindbender. A great middle ground for freeride skiing. Float is great, stability is good, dampness is adequate. Love the lively feel underfoot.

The Wildcat just feels alive underfoot. There's a springy energy to the maple core that makes every turn engaging. Great float in powder, good edge grip for a freeride ski, and enough pop for cliff drops. At 192cm it's a true big mountain tool. Moment nailed the construction.

Love how playful this ski is while still having backbone. You can throw switch airs and butters but it doesn't fold when you charge. The 108mm waist handles powder well. Edge grip is decent — not a carving ski but holds on traverses. Weight is moderate, manageable for long days.

Really fun freeride ski. Not as floaty as a dedicated powder board but much better on mixed conditions. The Moment quality and feel is excellent — lively, responsive, engaging. Stiffness is moderate — charges well but you feel the limits at very high speed. Great for 80% of skiing.

The Ripstick 96 is the perfect width for all-mountain skiing. Wide enough for light powder, narrow enough for quick carving on groomers. Elan's carbon construction keeps weight down. Decent playfulness and edge grip. Not exceptional in any one area but good at everything. A true quiver-of-one.

Used these for two weeks in the Dolomites. Great on the groomed pistes, decent in the off-piste snow. At 96mm they're right in the sweet spot. Light and fun to ski. Edge grip is good but not outstanding. Not as damp as heavier titanal skis. A good versatile choice.

Moved from beginner skis to the Ripstick 96 and it's a great progression ski. Forgiving enough that I'm not scared but capable enough to push me. Good edge grip, decent float in light powder. Playful and fun. The 96mm waist is a versatile width.

I only ski one week per year and need one ski for everything. The Ripstick 96 works great. Light enough that my legs don't die, wide enough for any condition, and forgiving enough for my rusty technique. Not a hard charger but that's not me anyway.

Good all-mountain option that leans slightly toward the playful side. The carbon Amphibio construction is unique — different flex on each edge for easier turn initiation. Not as stable as full titanal skis at speed but lighter and more maneuverable. Good value.

Solid advanced frontside ski. The 82mm waist is wide enough for some versatility but narrow enough for real carving. Titanal construction gives good dampness and edge grip. Stiff enough for aggressive skiing. The tip rocker helps in soft snow but it's really a piste ski.

Fischer makes excellent skis and the RC One 82 is no exception. Great edge grip on hardpack, stable at speed, damp ride. The plate binding adds precision. Available in many lengths including 182cm for tall skiers. Not a powder ski but handles light snow fine. My daily driver on the East Coast.

Very good carving ski. Edge grip is strong, the titanal layers keep it damp, and the camber with tip rocker gives good versatility. Not as edgy as a pure 70mm carver but the 82mm width adds a touch of stability. Stiff but not race-stiff. A great choice for strong intermediates to experts.

Recommended by the club and it delivers. Precise on groomers, good edge grip on hard snow, stable at speed. The tip rocker helps when it gets chopped up. At 82mm it's slightly wider than pure carvers which I appreciate for versatility. Not playful — this is a serious skiing tool.

Love this ski on corduroy. The combination of titanal dampness, plate precision, and 82mm width is perfect for all-day piste skiing. Not tiring like a race ski but still performs at a high level. Edge grip in icy conditions is excellent. Forgiveness is moderate — you need decent technique.

Perfect ski for my level. The K2 Disruption 78C is forgiving enough for my mistakes but has enough edge grip for confident turns. The rocker-camber profile helps with turn initiation. Lightweight, not tiring. Good on groomed runs. Gets a bit nervous on ice and at speed.

Bought these after lessons and they're great for practicing. Soft enough to be friendly, good enough edge grip for basic carving. The 78mm waist is perfect for piste skiing. Lightweight and easy to turn. I can feel the limits when I pick up speed but for now they're ideal.

Good ski for weekend family trips. Doesn't demand too much energy or technique. Easy turns, reasonable edge grip, light weight. The 78mm waist keeps things simple on groomed terrain. Not a ski for powder or ice but fine for regular resort conditions.

Found these at a swap and they're great for my intermediate level. Forgiving flex, decent grip, light and easy to ski. The composite cap construction isn't as damp as titanal but it doesn't need to be at the speeds I'm going. Good value, good ski.

I mostly stick to blue runs and these are perfect. Easy to turn, forgiving when I make a mistake, and light enough for all day. Don't take them to black diamonds — they get overwhelmed. But for intermediate groomed skiing, excellent choice.

Literally my first day skiing and these were so easy to use. Super soft flex, very light, and the rocker tip makes turning almost automatic. I was doing green runs by afternoon. Very forgiving — even when I lean back they don't punish me. Perfect for beginners.

These skis gave me confidence on my first trip. So easy to turn, so light, so forgiving. The short lengths (I'm on 149cm) are very manageable. On smooth groomed runs they're wonderful. They're also available in shorter sizes for petite skiers. Zero edge grip on ice but I avoid that anyway.

Excellent true beginner ski. The softest flex in our demo fleet. The rocker-camber profile makes it nearly impossible to catch an edge. Very light weight helps with fatigue. Students progress quickly on these. Limited to groomed terrain at slow speeds — that's exactly right for beginners.

I ski once a year and I need the easiest ski possible. These are it. Zero effort to turn, very forgiving, light as a feather. They're not exciting and they have no performance at speed but that doesn't matter for me. Good on groomed, terrible everywhere else.

Bought these to keep up with my kids on easy runs. They're the most user-friendly ski I've ever tried. Soft, light, forgiving, easy to turn. The short sizes (143cm!) work great for shorter/lighter skiers. Not a long-term ski — you'll outgrow them — but for getting started they're unbeatable.

The ARV 106 is the ultimate park-to-powder ski. Twin-tip design for switch riding, soft enough for jibbing but stiff enough for landings. The 106mm waist floats in powder and the rocker profile is park-tuned. Most playful ski I've ever been on — butters, spins, everything feels easy.

My go-to park ski. The poplar/ash core has incredible pop for airs and great dampness for landings. True twin shape means riding switch is natural. Rocker-camber-rocker profile handles everything. Edge grip is meh but who cares in the park. Playful factor is off the charts.

Armada makes the best freestyle skis and the ARV 106 proves it. Butter bar champion, spin machine, float machine. At 106mm it handles powder better than narrow park skis. The forgiving flex means you can land sketchy without getting thrown. Not a carving or speed ski at all.

Rides switch as well as forward. The true twin tip/tail profile with rocker is perfectly symmetrical. Great for laps in the park, fun for exploring off-piste with tricks. Edge grip is sacrificed for playfulness and that's fine. Float in powder is a bonus — many park skis sink.

Not the fastest or grippiest ski but the most fun. Every run is an adventure — butters, jibs, drops, switch. The ARV 106 encourages creativity. Floats decently in powder. On hardpack groomers it's not inspiring but it's tolerable. Weight is average, construction is solid.

Many of my advanced junior skiers ride the ARV. It's playful, forgiving for landings, and wide enough for all conditions. Not appropriate for racing or hard carving. Stability at speed is mediocre. But for developing all-mountain freestyle skills, it's excellent.

The Mantra M6 is legendary for a reason. This ski is an absolute tank — stiff, damp, and incredibly stable at any speed. The double titanal layers make it the most damp all-mountain ski available. Edge grip is outstanding for a 96mm waist ski. Demands strong technique. Not for the faint of heart.

If you ski hard and fast in all conditions, the Mantra is THE ski. Incredibly stable in chop, crud, wind-blown garbage — it just plows through everything. Damp beyond belief. Edge grip on hardpack is nearly carving-ski level. The 96mm waist handles powder adequately. Stiff, heavy, powerful.

The Mantra M6 sacrifices lightness and playfulness for raw power and stability. At 2050g it's one of the heaviest all-mountain skis but every gram translates to dampness and composure. Edge grip is exceptional. Not forgiving — this is an expert-only ski. Available up to 191cm.

Nothing handles crud like the Mantra. The weight and stiffness blast through the worst snow conditions. My confidence ski for end-of-day chop. Edge grip on ice is remarkable for the width. Stability at high speed is 10/10. Playfulness is low — it's a serious tool for serious skiers.

Compared the Mantra M6 to the Enforcer 100 and Brahma 88. The Mantra is by far the most stable and damp. It's also by far the most demanding. The Enforcer is more versatile, the Brahma more nimble. The Mantra is for experts who prioritize performance over comfort. Camber with tip rocker profile.

This ski laughs at ice. The edge grip from the titanal construction and camber profile is insane. I've never felt more secure on hardpack. Combined with the 96mm for occasional off-piste adventures, the Mantra is the ultimate East Coast expert all-mountain ski. Heavy but worth every gram.

The Santa Ana 98 is my quiver-of-one. At 98mm it handles a good range of conditions. The titanal construction gives it surprising dampness and edge grip for a lighter all-mountain ski. Stability is good, playfulness is moderate. Works well in shorter sizes for lighter skiers.

Good all-mountain ski that handles Pacific Northwest conditions well. Enough float for our wetter powder, enough grip for our variable groomers. Not as powerful as the Enforcer but lighter and more maneuverable. Damp and composed for its weight class. The 172cm works well.

Demoed the Santa Ana alongside the Enforcer and Ripstick. It sits right between them — less powerful than the Enforcer, more stable than the Ripstick. The 98mm waist is a great all-purpose width. Edge grip is good, playfulness is moderate. A solid, unspectacular choice.

Perfect for someone who skis 20-30 days a year. Handles everything I encounter without being too demanding. The 98mm waist, titanal construction, and rocker-camber-rocker profile add up to a very versatile ski. Damp enough for crud, grippy enough for groomers, floaty enough for powder.

Love that this comes in 151cm! Most all-mountain skis in this category start at 165+. The shorter sizes are great for lightweight and shorter skiers. Performance doesn't suffer — still damp, stable, and versatile. Edge grip is solid. My favorite all-arounder.

The Stance 90 is Salomon's best-kept secret. At 90mm waist with camber and tip rocker, it carves groomers beautifully while having enough width for light off-piste. Titanal construction makes it damp and stable. Edge grip is very strong. Not as playful as wider all-mountain skis but more precise.

Great ski for people who mostly carve groomers but occasionally venture off-piste. The 90mm waist is the right width for this balance. Stiff and damp from the titanal construction. Edge grip on hardpack is excellent. Float in powder is limited but acceptable for light days.

On the East Coast, edge grip is everything and the Stance 90 delivers. The camber profile with just tip rocker means excellent grip on our icy conditions. Damp and stable, doesn't chatter on hardpack. The 90mm waist adds just enough versatility beyond a pure carving ski. My daily driver.

The 188cm is available and works great for my 6'2" frame. Good stability at speed, solid edge grip, damp ride. The Stance 90 sits between a carving ski and an all-mountain ski — a frontside all-mountain if you will. Not for deep powder but handles everything else well.

First chair on a freshly groomed day with the Stance 90 is heaven. Edge grip is incredible, the carving feel is precise, and the titanal dampness smooths everything out. Once things get chopped up I'm less enthusiastic but those early morning laps are pure gold. Not playful, very precise.

The Black Ops Sender Ti is a big-mountain beast. Rossignol loaded it with titanal and the result is maximum dampness and stability. At 106mm with rocker it floats well. But the real magic is in gnarly conditions — it powers through anything. Stiff, heavy, and incredibly composed.

Built for steep terrain and variable conditions. The Sender Ti is one of the stiffest freeride skis available. Dampness is exceptional — you feel nothing but smooth power. Edge grip is strong for the width. Not playful at all but that's fine when you're dropping 50-degree couloirs.

My weapon for Chamonix's extreme terrain. The 192cm handles big speeds and big drops. Incredibly damp construction absorbs everything. Stability at speed is class-leading. The weight is brutal on bootpacks but the descent makes up for it. A specialist's ski for serious terrain.

The Sender Ti is for experts who ski the gnarliest terrain. Pros: incredible dampness, stability, and power. Cons: heavy, not playful, demanding technique. The 106mm waist provides good float but this ski is really about charging hard terrain. Not an everyday ski for most people.

I use these on the most demanding patrol days. When the terrain is bombed-out, wind-affected, or avalanche debris, the Sender Ti handles it all. Stiff, damp, and stable beyond anything else I've used. Edge grip on firm traverses is excellent. Not a fun playful ski — it's a work tool.

The Line Blade is a hidden gem. Maple and metal construction gives it a unique combo of liveliness and dampness. Edge grip is outstanding — just below race level. At 72mm waist it's dedicated to carving. The camber profile maximizes edge contact. More lively than the Supershape, equally precise.

Incredible edge grip. The Blade lives up to its name — on hardpack it cuts like a knife. Stiff flex, stable at speed, damp enough to smooth out imperfections. The 72mm waist means rapid edge-to-edge transitions. Not a ski for powder or off-piste. Pure carving excellence.

The most fun carving ski I've tried. Where the Supershape is clinical, the Blade has personality. The maple core gives it a lively, snappy feel that makes carving addictive. Edge grip is world-class. Stability is excellent. Slightly more playful than other expert carvers which I love.

Demoed the Blade against the Supershape and Deacon. Edge grip is comparable to both. The Blade is the most lively of the three — it has a spring to it that makes you want to carve harder. Dampness is a tick below the Supershape. Stability is excellent. Pure on-piste. Camber profile, no rocker at all.

If you love pure carving, the Blade delivers. Outstanding edge grip, stiff but not brutal, and that lively maple core makes it engaging. At 72mm waist it's razor-quick edge to edge. No rocker means no forgiveness in soft snow but maximum grip on hardpack. Available up to 181cm.

The Dictator 3.0 is Faction's all-mountain freeride offering and it's excellent. Poplar/paulownia core keeps weight down while providing good snap. Very playful — great for butters, natural features, and creative skiing. At 106mm it floats well in powder. Edge grip is acceptable. Fun factor is sky high.

This ski encourages you to find creative lines everywhere. The playfulness is addictive — you start looking for features to hit on every run. Good float in powder, decent stability for moderate speeds. Not a charger — it prefers medium speed with lots of style. The construction feels poppy and energetic.

A fun-oriented freeride ski that doesn't take itself too seriously. More playful than the Enforcer or Mindbender, less stable at speed. The 106mm waist handles powder well. Edge grip is adequate. Dampness is moderate — you'll feel chop more than with titanal skis. Great for style-focused skiers.

I wanted a ski that can ride switch and handle off-piste terrain. The Dictator 3.0 does both well. The rocker profile and playful flex let me ride switch comfortably. Float in powder is good. It won't charge like a Mindbender but I'm having too much fun to notice. Available up to 190cm.

Good option for freeride skiing with a playful emphasis. Not as stable or damp as heavier options but more fun in tight terrain and for creative skiing. The poplar/paulownia construction keeps it light and snappy. Float is good, edge grip is fine, forgiveness is above average.

The Brahma 88 is Blizzard's best-kept secret. At 88mm with titanal construction, it carves groomers with authority while having enough width for mixed conditions. Incredibly damp — smoothest ride under 90mm. Edge grip is stellar. Stable at any speed. Not playful but very competent.

The Brahma sits between a carving ski and an all-mountain ski and absolutely nails it. At 88mm waist it's frontside-focused but handles off-piste when needed. The titanal construction gives it dampness that rivals heavier skis. Edge grip on hardpack is excellent. Stable, precise, confidence-inspiring.

On the East Coast the Brahma 88 is king. Edge grip on our icy conditions is phenomenal. The titanal dampness smooths out the chatter. 88mm is wide enough for occasional off-piste but narrow enough for real carving. Stable at speed, doesn't wander. My one-ski recommendation for East Coast experts.

Excellent groomer ski with all-mountain capability. The Brahma 88 has titanal-level dampness and edge grip in a relatively narrow package. Carves groomers beautifully, handles crud competently, struggles only in deep powder. The tip rocker helps with versatility. Not playful — it's a precision tool.

Head-to-head vs Stance 90, RC One 82, and Mantra M6: The Brahma 88 is the most balanced of the group. Not as narrow/precise as the Fischer, not as wide/demanding as the Mantra, not as carving-focused as the Stance. It does everything well. Edge grip and dampness are top-tier.

The Brahma's edge grip on hardpack is second to none in the all-mountain category. The double titanal layers create a damp, composed ride that inspires confidence on ice. At 88mm the carving feel is close to a narrower frontside ski. Camber with tip rocker is the right profile. Not forgiving for beginners.
